findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who experience this pain — add a slide showing 3-4 specific customer conversations (even brief ones) that validate the problem, like "Spoke with 12 villa developers in Dubai; 8 cited reliability as their #1 blocker to solar adoption" or "Collected 6 letters of intent from property managers willing to pilot SolarisKit." This doesn't need to be a massive study — just concrete evidence that real buyers confirmed your problem statement before you built the solution.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a timeline—add a specific regulatory deadline or enforcement date (like "Estidama compliance required by Q3 2025 for all new villa permits") and tie it directly to when developers will actually need your solution, so investors see this isn't just a nice-to-have but a forced buying moment happening right now.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads as a feature roadmap, not a defensible technology. Add one concrete example showing how your AI actually works: for instance, "HelioHelp ingests building orientation, roof load capacity, and local water hardness data, then outputs a custom collector array configuration and predicted 25-year performance curve—something installers previously needed 3-5 days of engineering to calculate." This shows investors the real intelligence layer, not just automation.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You've built HelioHelp as your scaling engine, but investors will want to know what sits underneath it — are you building the AI models from scratch, using a third-party LLM like OpenAI or Claude, relying on cloud infrastructure like AWS or Google Cloud, or pulling in open-source components? Add a single sentence to Slide 19 that names your core dependencies (e.g., "HelioHelp runs on [cloud provider] infrastructure with [LLM/model] powering the AI layer"), so investors understand your cost structure and what happens if a key vendor changes pricing or terms.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in the GCC and Abu Dhabi with mandated solar regulations, so investors will want to know how you're handling data privacy, cybersecurity, and local compliance — especially since HelioHelp collects building details and performance data from installations. Add a brief section to your Technology or Appendix slide stating your data governance approach: for example, "HelioHelp operates under GDPR and UAE Data Protection Law; customer data stored in-region on secure servers; no third-party sharing without consent" — this removes a compliance question before it gets asked and signals you've thought operationally.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-line assumptions box to your financials slide that spells out the math behind your Base case: something like "Base case assumes: 50 units 2026 → 150 units 2027 (3x growth via developer partnerships) → 300 units 2028; $4,100 ASP hardware + $800 installation labor; 40% COGS." Right now investors see $1.05M revenue in 2027 but have no idea if that's 100 units at $10.5K each or 500 units at $2.1K each—making it impossible for them to stress-test your logic or believe the trajectory.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand if your unit economics actually work at scale—right now it looks like you're selling one-off projects, not a repeatable business. Add a simple box showing: for a typical hotel customer, what's your customer acquisition cost (sales/marketing spend per deal), what's the lifetime value (total O&M + licensing revenue over 5-7 years), and the ratio—for example, "Hotel CAC: $8K | Hotel LTV: $120K | LTV:CAC: 15x" would show investors this is a

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 14, your 2026 hardware revenue of $0.3M doesn't match the $213K in contracted deals shown on Slide 12—that's a $87K gap that will confuse investors about what's actually locked in versus projected. Clarify by adding a footnote to the 2026 line that breaks down: "$213K from Rixos + EtihadWE (contracted, Q2 delivery) + $87K from [specific pipeline deals expected to close in 2026]" so investors see exactly which opportunities bridge the gap between your signed contracts and your revenue forecast.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy this new funding round—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing scale-up: $X to expand Ras Al Khaimah facility," "Sales & BD team: $X for 3 hires," "Working capital for Rixos + EtihadWE deployment: $X," "R&D for HelioFlow v2: $X"). Right now investors see your $350K seed runway and your 2026 revenue targets, but they don't know what *this* round is for or how it directly unlocks those milestones—make that connection explicit and concrete.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under Rixos and EtihadWE stating the payment status and approximate revenue received—something like "Rixos: $180K revenue received Q3 2024" or "EtihadWE: Live installation, $45K paid to date"—so investors see actual cash in the door, not just project names.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue movement—add a simple chart showing your monthly revenue for the last 6-8 months (even if some months are zero) so they can see the trajectory you're building, like "Month 1: $0 → Month 3: $12K → Month 6: $47K" alongside your Rixos and EtihadWE projects. This transforms your slide from potential into proof.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: Add a single metric to your Rixos case study showing the system has stayed operational and performing since October 2024—something like "12+ months uptime, zero maintenance calls" or "system availability: 99.2%"—to prove your reliability claims aren't just engineering theory but proven in the field where it matters most to hotel operators.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Replace "Legacy Solar," "Heat Pumps," and "Electric Storage" with actual competitor names—something like "Sunrun Solar," "Carrier Heat Pumps," and "Tesla Powerwall"—so investors immediately understand who you're beating and can validate your claims against real products they know. Right now the table reads as theoretical; naming competitors makes it credible and memorable.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why you're better than existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Daikin, or a regional solar giant decides to build exactly what you're building—they have distribution, capital, and brand trust you don't. Add a slide that names 1-2 specific incumbents, explains why they haven't solved this yet (e.g., "Heat pump makers optimize for cold climates, not GCC's 45°C+ peaks, so their R&D roadmaps don't prioritize this"), and articulate your defensibility (patents on the polymer absorber design, first-mover advantage in GCC market, or exclusive partnerships

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing a layer of credibility that investors care about: who else believes in this? Add a small "Advisors" section below your team that includes 2-3 people with relevant expertise—ideally someone with solar/cleantech manufacturing scale experience, someone with distribution/partnership expertise in your target markets, and maybe a former founder or operator who's exited successfully. For example: "Dr. Ahmed Al-Mansouri, former VP Manufacturing at [major solar company], advising on production scale-up" would immediately signal you've thought about execution risks and have someone in your corner who's done this before.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders and any equity cliffs—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" next to his bio. This shows investors you've thought through retention incentives and aren't planning a quick exit, which builds confidence in your execution timeline.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification mentioned on Slide 3, but investors in regulated markets need to see the full compliance picture upfront—add a dedicated compliance slide before the financials that maps which specific UAE building codes (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel) your system meets, what certifications you hold or are pursuing (electrical safety, pressure equipment, etc.), and your timeline to any remaining approvals, since tightening regulations are listed as a market driver but your regulatory readiness isn't clear. This turns a potential investor concern into proof that you've already solved the compliance puzzle competitors will face.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked in three jurisdictions and a strong IP moat on the slide, but investors will want to know you've done the legal homework on the other side—add a brief statement confirming no pending litigation, IP disputes, or encumbrances exist, ideally positioned on your risk management slide (Appendix B) or as a single line in your legal/compliance section. Something like "No pending litigation, IP disputes, or regulatory encumbrances; all patents and trademarks clear and enforceable" signals you've vetted this and removes a question mark that will come up in due diligence anyway.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these match. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing the math that connects your 5% UAE market share assumption to the $400M SAM figure—something like "5% of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" so investors can instantly see how your near-term target flows from your market sizing, rather than appearing as two disconnected numbers.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.suggestion: I don't see any professional presentation issues in your deck—formatting is clean, there are no spelling errors I can spot, and no placeholder text. You're in good shape on this front. If the flag came up as "NOT_FOUND," it means the system didn't detect a problem, which is actually the right outcome. Keep the deck as is and focus your energy on the gaps that matter more to investors right now.
